title: The Tangent and Velocity Problems
objectives: Calculate the average rate of change of a function over an interval and interpret it as a slope
Understand the geometric connection between secant lines and average rate of change
Compute the slope of a tangent line using the limit of secant slopes as the interval shrinks
Apply instantaneous rate of change to solve velocity problems in physics

devotional.title: A Time for Every Matter
devotional.content: "For everything there is a season, and a time for every matter under heaven—a time to be born, and a time to die; a time to plant, and a time to pluck up what is planted." The Preacher understood that life moves through time at varying rates. A seed planted in spring grows slowly at first, then rapidly, then slows as it matures. The calculus we develop here—measuring rates of change at a single instant—gives us the mathematical language to describe how quickly things change *right now*, not just on average. When we study the tangent line to a curve, we glimpse the same wisdom Solomon knew: that every moment has its own character, its own rate of becoming. God, who exists outside of time, sees all rates of change at once. We, creatures bound in time, must calculate them step by step—and in doing so, we learn to see creation with greater clarity.

content.text: How fast is a stone falling at the very instant it strikes the ground? How quickly is a population growing *right now*, not over the last decade but at this precise moment? These questions—deceptively simple to ask—launched one of the greatest intellectual revolutions in history. To answer them, we need a concept that does not yet exist in our mathematical vocabulary: the *limit*. But before we build that powerful tool, let us start with something entirely familiar.
Average Rate of Change

You have been computing rates of change since algebra. When you divide the distance traveled by the time elapsed, you get an average speed. When you compute the slope of a line through two points, you measure how fast $y$ changes relative to $x$. The idea is the same every time: *how much does the output change per unit of input?*

content.title: Average Rate of Change
content.content:
Let $f$ be a function defined on an interval containing $a$ and $b$ with $a \neq b$. The **average rate of change** of $f$ from $a$ to $b$ is

$$\text{average rate of change} = \frac{f(b) - f(a)}{b - a} = \frac{\Delta y}{\Delta x},$$

where $\Delta y = f(b) - f(a)$ denotes the change in the output and $\Delta x = b - a$ denotes the change in the input.

content.text: There is nothing exotic here. This is the same "rise over run" you learned when you first studied linear functions. The only difference is that now the function need not be linear, so the rate of change depends on which two input values you choose.

content.title: Average speed on a road trip
content.problem: A family drives from Phoenix to San Diego. At 10:00 AM their odometer reads 42,310 miles; at 1:00 PM it reads 42,490 miles. What was their average speed?
content.solution:
We have $\Delta\text{distance} = 42{,}490 - 42{,}310 = 180$ miles and $\Delta\text{time} = 3$ hours, so

$$\text{average speed} = \frac{180 \text{ mi}}{3 \text{ hr}} = 60 \text{ mi/hr}.$$

Of course the car did not travel at exactly 60 mph every second. It slowed for traffic, accelerated on the freeway, and stopped at a rest area. The average rate of change tells us the *overall* rate, smoothing out all the variation.

content.title: Average rate of change of a quadratic
content.problem:
Let $f(x) = x^{2}$. Find the average rate of change of $f$ on each interval.

**(a)** From $x = 1$ to $x = 3$

**(b)** From $x = 1$ to $x = 2$

**(c)** From $x = 1$ to $x = 1.5$
content.solution:
We apply the formula $\dfrac{f(b)-f(a)}{b-a}$ in each case.

**(a)** $\displaystyle\frac{f(3)-f(1)}{3-1} = \frac{9-1}{2} = \frac{8}{2} = 4.$

**(b)** $\displaystyle\frac{f(2)-f(1)}{2-1} = \frac{4-1}{1} = 3.$

**(c)** $\displaystyle\frac{f(1.5)-f(1)}{1.5-1} = \frac{2.25-1}{0.5} = \frac{1.25}{0.5} = 2.5.$

Notice that the average rate of change *decreases* as we shrink the interval toward $x=1$. The values $4, 3, 2.5$ seem to be approaching something—but what? We shall return to this question shortly.

content.title: Average rate of change from a table
content.problem:
The table below records the temperature $T$ (in degrees Fahrenheit) at various times $t$ (in hours after midnight) on a January morning.

| $t$ (hours) | 0 | 2 | 4 | 6 | 8 | 10 |
|-------------|---|---|---|---|---|----|
| $T(t)$ (°F) | 38 | 35 | 31 | 28 | 33 | 42 |

Find the average rate of change of temperature from $t=0$ to $t=6$, and from $t=6$ to $t=10$.
content.solution:
From $t=0$ to $t=6$:

$$\frac{T(6)-T(0)}{6-0} = \frac{28-38}{6} = \frac{-10}{6} \approx -1.67\;°\text{F/hr}.$$

The negative sign tells us the temperature was *falling*—on average, about $1.67$ degrees per hour.

From $t=6$ to $t=10$:

$$\frac{T(10)-T(6)}{10-6} = \frac{42-28}{4} = \frac{14}{4} = 3.5\;°\text{F/hr}.$$

After sunrise the temperature rose at an average rate of $3.5$ degrees per hour.

content.text: The Secant Line: A Geometric Picture

Now let us look at what the average rate of change means *geometrically*. Consider the graph of a function $y = f(x)$ and two points on it: $P = \bigl(a,\, f(a)\bigr)$ and $Q = \bigl(b,\, f(b)\bigr)$. The line through $P$ and $Q$ is called a **secant line**.

content.title: Secant Line
content.content:
A **secant line** to the graph of $f$ is a line passing through two distinct points $\bigl(a, f(a)\bigr)$ and $\bigl(b, f(b)\bigr)$ on the graph. Its slope is the average rate of change of $f$ from $a$ to $b$:

$$m_{\mathrm{sec}} = \frac{f(b) - f(a)}{b - a}.$$

content.text: Notice: this is not a new formula. It is the *same* quotient from Definition 2.1, now recognized as the slope of a line. Every time you have computed an average rate of change, you have been computing the slope of a secant line—you simply did not call it that.

content.title: Equation of a secant line
content.problem: Let $f(x) = \sqrt{x}$. Find the equation of the secant line through the points where $x = 1$ and $x = 4$.
content.solution:
The two points are $P = (1, 1)$ and $Q = (4, 2)$. The slope is

$$m_{\mathrm{sec}} = \frac{2 - 1}{4 - 1} = \frac{1}{3}.$$

Using the point-slope form with the point $(1, 1)$:

$$y - 1 = \tfrac{1}{3}(x - 1) \quad\Longrightarrow\quad y = \tfrac{1}{3}x + \tfrac{2}{3}.$$

content.text: The Tangent Problem
A secant line captures the average behavior of $f$ between two points. But what if we want to know how $f$ is behaving at a *single* point? What is the slope of the curve *right there*?

content.title: Archimedes of Syracuse (c. 287–212 BC)
content.content: The Greek mathematician Archimedes found tangent lines to spirals more than two thousand years ago using an ingenious method of exhaustion. His approach—approximating a curved figure with ever-finer straight-line segments—anticipates the limit process we develop here. Archimedes did not have the formal language of limits, but his geometric intuition was remarkably close to the truth that Newton and Leibniz would formalize centuries later.

content.text: To find the slope of a curve at a single point $P$, we take a second point $Q$ on the curve and draw the secant line $PQ$. Then we slide $Q$ closer and closer to $P$. As $Q$ approaches $P$, the secant line rotates and—if all goes well—settles into a fixed position. That limiting position is the **tangent line** at $P$.

content.title: Tangent Line (Informal)
content.content:
The **tangent line** to the curve $y = f(x)$ at the point $P = \bigl(a, f(a)\bigr)$ is the line through $P$ whose slope is

$$m_{\mathrm{tan}} = \lim_{x \to a} \frac{f(x) - f(a)}{x - a},$$

provided this limit exists. Equivalently, writing $x = a + h$,

$$m_{\mathrm{tan}} = \lim_{h \to 0}\, \frac{f(a+h) - f(a)}{h}.$$

We will make the word "limit" precise in Section 2.2. For now, interpret it as: *the value that the expression approaches as $h$ gets arbitrarily close to $0$*.

content.text: Let us see this in action with a concrete calculation.

content.title: Tangent slope by shrinking the interval
content.problem: Estimate the slope of the tangent line to $f(x) = x^{2}$ at the point $(1,1)$ by computing secant slopes for smaller and smaller values of $h$.

content.title: Tangent line to a cubic
content.problem: Find the slope of the tangent line to $f(x) = x^{3}$ at the point $(2, 8)$.
content.solution:
We form the difference quotient:

$$\frac{f(2+h) - f(2)}{h} = \frac{(2+h)^{3} - 8}{h}.$$

Expanding $(2+h)^{3} = 8 + 12h + 6h^{2} + h^{3}$, we obtain

$$\frac{8 + 12h + 6h^{2} + h^{3} - 8}{h} = \frac{12h + 6h^{2} + h^{3}}{h} = 12 + 6h + h^{2}.$$

As $h \to 0$, this expression approaches $12$. Therefore

$$m_{\mathrm{tan}} = 12,$$

and the tangent line is $y - 8 = 12(x-2)$, or equivalently $y = 12x - 16$.

content.title: The tangent is not always "touching once"
content.content: In elementary geometry you learned that a tangent to a circle touches the circle at exactly one point. For general curves, this is *not* a valid definition. A tangent line may cross the curve elsewhere, and some lines that touch a curve at a single point are not tangent lines. The correct definition involves the limit process above.

content.text: The Velocity Problem
The tangent problem asks: *What is the slope of a curve at a point?* The velocity problem asks the same question in a physical setting: *How fast is an object moving at a particular instant?*

Suppose an object moves along a straight line, and its position at time $t$ is given by a function $s(t)$. Over a time interval from $t = a$ to $t = a + h$, the object's displacement is $s(a+h) - s(a)$, and the elapsed time is $h$.

content.title: Average and Instantaneous Velocity
content.content:
The **average velocity** of an object with position function $s(t)$ over the interval $[a,\, a+h]$ is

$$v_{\mathrm{avg}} = \frac{s(a+h) - s(a)}{h} = \frac{\Delta s}{\Delta t}.$$

The **instantaneous velocity** at time $t = a$ is

$$v(a) = \lim_{h \to 0}\, \frac{s(a+h) - s(a)}{h},$$

provided the limit exists.

content.text: Compare this with Definition 2.3. The formulas are identical! The tangent problem and the velocity problem are the *same* mathematical problem dressed in different clothing. This unification is one of the great insights of calculus.

content.title: A ball thrown upward
content.problem:
A ball is thrown straight up from a bridge 48 feet above a river, with an initial velocity of $64$ ft/s. Neglecting air resistance, its height after $t$ seconds is

$$s(t) = -16t^{2} + 64t + 48 \quad\text{(feet)}.$$

**(a)** Find the average velocity over $[1, 3]$.

**(b)** Find the average velocity over $[1, 1.5]$.

**(c)** Find the instantaneous velocity at $t = 1$.
content.solution:
**(a)** We compute $s(1) = -16 + 64 + 48 = 96$ and $s(3) = -16(9) + 64(3) + 48 = -144 + 192 + 48 = 96$. Therefore

$$v_{\mathrm{avg}} = \frac{s(3)-s(1)}{3-1} = \frac{96-96}{2} = 0 \;\text{ft/s}.$$

An average velocity of zero! The ball is at the same height at $t=1$ and $t=3$—it went up and came back down.

**(b)** $s(1.5) = -16(2.25) + 64(1.5) + 48 = -36 + 96 + 48 = 108$. So

$$v_{\mathrm{avg}} = \frac{s(1.5)-s(1)}{0.5} = \frac{108-96}{0.5} = \frac{12}{0.5} = 24 \;\text{ft/s}.$$

**(c)** We compute the difference quotient:

$$\frac{s(1+h)-s(1)}{h} = \frac{\bigl[-16(1+h)^{2}+64(1+h)+48\bigr]-96}{h}.$$

Expanding:

$$\begin{aligned} s(1+h) &= -16(1 + 2h + h^{2}) + 64 + 64h + 48 \\ &= -16 - 32h - 16h^{2} + 64 + 64h + 48 \\ &= 96 + 32h - 16h^{2}. \end{aligned}$$

Therefore

$$\frac{s(1+h)-s(1)}{h} = \frac{96 + 32h - 16h^{2} - 96}{h} = \frac{32h - 16h^{2}}{h} = 32 - 16h.$$

As $h \to 0$:

$$v(1) = \lim_{h \to 0}\,(32-16h) = 32 \;\text{ft/s}.$$

At exactly $t = 1$ second, the ball is rising at 32 ft/s.

content.title: When does the ball reach its peak?
content.problem: Using the position function from Example 2.7, find the instant when the ball reaches its maximum height.
content.solution:
At the peak, the ball is momentarily stationary: its instantaneous velocity is zero. We compute the velocity at a general time $t = a$:

$$\frac{s(a+h)-s(a)}{h} = \frac{\bigl[-16(a+h)^{2}+64(a+h)+48\bigr] - \bigl[-16a^{2}+64a+48\bigr]}{h}.$$

Expanding and simplifying (the $-16a^{2}$, $64a$, and $48$ terms cancel):

$$= \frac{-32ah - 16h^{2} + 64h}{h} = -32a - 16h + 64.$$

Taking the limit as $h \to 0$:

$$v(a) = -32a + 64.$$

Setting $v(a) = 0$: $-32a + 64 = 0 \;\Longrightarrow\; a = 2$. The ball reaches its peak at $t = 2$ seconds. The maximum height is $s(2) = -16(4) + 64(2) + 48 = 112$ feet.

content.text: Preview: The Need for Limits

Both the tangent problem and the velocity problem led us to the same expression:

$$\lim_{h \to 0}\,\frac{f(a+h)-f(a)}{h}.$$

In our examples, we simplified the fraction algebraically, cancelled the $h$ in the denominator, and then "set $h$ equal to zero" in what remained. But this informal approach conceals deep questions. What if the algebra does not simplify so neatly? What *exactly* do we mean by "the value the expression approaches"?

content.title: Computing Tangent Slopes (for now)
content.content:
To find the slope of the tangent line to $y = f(x)$ at $x = a$:

1. Form the difference quotient $\displaystyle\frac{f(a+h)-f(a)}{h}$.
2. Simplify algebraically until you can cancel the $h$ in the denominator.
3. Evaluate the simplified expression at $h = 0$ to obtain $m_{\mathrm{tan}}$.
4. Write the tangent line: $y - f(a) = m_{\mathrm{tan}}(x - a)$.

In Section 2.2, we will replace step 3 with a rigorous definition of the limit.

content.title: Tangent to a rational function
content.problem: Find the slope of the tangent line to $\displaystyle f(x) = \frac{1}{x}$ at the point $\bigl(2, \tfrac{1}{2}\bigr)$.
content.solution:
The difference quotient is

$$\frac{f(2+h)-f(2)}{h} = \frac{\dfrac{1}{2+h} - \dfrac{1}{2}}{h}.$$

Combining the fractions in the numerator:

$$= \frac{\;\dfrac{2 - (2+h)}{2(2+h)}\;}{h} = \frac{-h}{2h(2+h)} = \frac{-1}{2(2+h)}.$$

As $h \to 0$:

$$m_{\mathrm{tan}} = \frac{-1}{2(2+0)} = -\frac{1}{4}.$$

The tangent line at $\bigl(2,\tfrac{1}{2}\bigr)$ is

$$y - \tfrac{1}{2} = -\tfrac{1}{4}(x-2) \quad\Longrightarrow\quad y = -\tfrac{1}{4}x + 1.$$

content.title: Isaac Newton (1642–1727) and Gottfried Wilhelm Leibniz (1646–1716)
content.content:
The tangent problem and the velocity problem were central motivations for the invention of calculus. Newton, working in England, developed his "method of fluxions" in the mid-1660s, partly inspired by questions about planetary motion. Leibniz, working independently in Germany, created his own version of calculus in the 1670s with superior notation—including the $\frac{dy}{dx}$ and $\int$ symbols we still use today. Both men saw that the tangent problem and the area problem are *inverse* operations, a deep connection known as the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus (Chapter 6).

Newton was a devout, if unorthodox, Christian who wrote more pages of theology than mathematics. He saw his scientific work as revealing the designs of the Creator: "This most beautiful system of the sun, planets, and comets could only proceed from the counsel and dominion of an intelligent and powerful Being."

content.text: Exercises
Average Rate of Change

content.problem: Find the average rate of change of $f(x) = 3x + 5$ from $x = 1$ to $x = 4$.
Find the average rate of change of $f(x) = x^{2} - 2x$ from $x = 0$ to $x = 3$.
Find the average rate of change of $g(t) = t^{3}$ on the interval $[1, 4]$.
A car's position (in miles) along a highway is given by $s(t) = 3t^{2} + t$ where $t$ is in hours. Find the average velocity over the interval $[1, 3]$.
The population of a colony of bacteria (in thousands) at time $t$ hours is $P(t) = 2^{t}$. Find the average rate of growth from $t = 0$ to $t = 3$.
Find the average rate of change of $f(x) = \sqrt{x}$ from $x = 4$ to $x = 9$.
Find the average rate of change of $h(x) = \dfrac{1}{x}$ from $x = 1$ to $x = 5$.
The height (in feet) of a sunflower $t$ weeks after planting is modeled by $H(t) = \dfrac{120}{1 + 14e^{-0.5t}}$. Using a calculator, find the average growth rate from $t = 4$ to $t = 8$.
Find the average rate of change of $f(x) = x^{2} - 4x + 1$ on the interval $[a,\, a+h]$. Simplify your answer.
Find the average rate of change of $f(x) = x^{3}$ on the interval $[a,\, a+h]$. Simplify your answer.

content.text: Secant Lines

content.problem: Find the slope of the secant line to $f(x) = x^{2} + 1$ through the points where $x = 0$ and $x = 2$.
Find the equation of the secant line to $f(x) = \sqrt{x+1}$ through the points where $x = 0$ and $x = 3$.
For $f(x) = x^{2}$, find the slope of the secant line through $(1, f(1))$ and $(1+h, f(1+h))$ in terms of $h$. Simplify.

content.text: Tangent Lines

content.problem: Use the limit process to find the slope of the tangent line to $f(x) = 3x^{2}$ at $x = 1$. Then write the equation of the tangent line.
Find the slope of the tangent line to $f(x) = x^{2} + x$ at $x = 2$ by computing $\lim_{h \to 0}\dfrac{f(2+h)-f(2)}{h}$.
Find the equation of the tangent line to $f(x) = x^{2} - 3x$ at the point $(4, 4)$.
Find the equation of the tangent line to $f(x) = x^{3}$ at the point $(1, 1)$.
Find the slope of the tangent line to $f(x) = \dfrac{1}{x}$ at $x = 3$.
Find the equation of the tangent line to $f(x) = \sqrt{x}$ at $x = 4$. *Hint:* After forming the difference quotient, rationalize the numerator.

content.text: Velocity

content.problem: A stone is dropped from a 400-foot cliff. Its height after $t$ seconds is $s(t) = 400 - 16t^{2}$. Find the average velocity over $[2, 5]$.
Using the same position function from the previous problem, find the instantaneous velocity at $t = 3$.

A particle moves along a line with position $s(t) = t^{2} - 6t + 5$ (feet, seconds).

**(a)** Find the average velocity over $[1, 4]$.

**(b)** Find the instantaneous velocity at $t = 1$.

**(c)** At what time is the instantaneous velocity equal to zero?
An object's position is $s(t) = t^{3} - 3t$ meters at time $t$ seconds. Find the instantaneous velocity at $t = 2$.

content.text: Challenging Problems

content.problem: The graph of $f(x) = x^{2}$ has a tangent line at the point $(a, a^{2})$. Show that this tangent line has equation $y = 2ax - a^{2}$, and find the value of $a$ for which the tangent line passes through the point $(0, -9)$.
Find the slope of the tangent line to $f(x) = \dfrac{1}{x^{2}}$ at $x = a$ (where $a \neq 0$) by computing $\lim_{h \to 0}\dfrac{f(a+h)-f(a)}{h}$.

A ball is launched upward from ground level with initial velocity $v_{0}$. Its height is $s(t) = v_{0}t - 16t^{2}$.

**(a)** Find the instantaneous velocity at time $t = a$.

**(b)** At what time does the ball reach its peak?

**(c)** Show that the ball's speed when it returns to the ground equals its initial speed $v_{0}$.
Find the equation of the tangent line to $f(x) = \sqrt{x}$ at a general point $(a, \sqrt{a})$, $a > 0$. Use this to find the tangent line at $x = 9$.

Two cars start from the same point. Car A's position is $s_{A}(t) = t^{2} + 2t$ and Car B's is $s_{B}(t) = 3t$ (in miles, with $t$ in hours).

**(a)** At what time do they have the same average velocity over $[0, t]$?

**(b)** At what instant does Car A's instantaneous velocity first equal Car B's velocity?
Find the slope of the tangent line to $f(x) = x + \dfrac{1}{x}$ at $x = a$ ($a \neq 0$). For what value(s) of $a$ is the tangent line horizontal?
Consider $f(x) = |x|$. Compute $\dfrac{f(0+h)-f(0)}{h}$ for $h > 0$ and for $h < 0$ separately. What do you conclude about the tangent line to $f$ at $x = 0$?

A rock is thrown downward from a 200-foot tower with an initial speed of $20$ ft/s. Its height is $s(t) = 200 - 20t - 16t^{2}$.

**(a)** Find the instantaneous velocity at $t = 2$.

**(b)** At what time does the rock hit the ground? (*Hint:* Set $s(t) = 0$.)

**(c)** What is the rock's velocity at impact?

content.text: Proofs

content.problem: Let $f(x) = mx + b$ be a linear function. Show that the tangent line at any point $(a, f(a))$ is the line $y = f(x)$ itself. Why does this make intuitive sense?
Prove that the average rate of change of $f(x) = x^{2}$ on the interval $[a, b]$ equals the instantaneous rate of change at the midpoint $x = \frac{a+b}{2}$.
Let $f(x) = x^{2}+c$ for a constant $c$. Show that the slope of the tangent line at $x = a$ does not depend on $c$. Explain geometrically why this is true.

Let $f$ and $g$ be functions and define $s(x) = f(x) + g(x)$. Using the difference quotient, show that if the tangent slopes of $f$ and $g$ at $x = a$ both exist, then the tangent slope of $s$ at $x = a$ is the sum of the tangent slopes. (You may assume that limits "respect addition.")

content.text: Creation Reveals

content.problem:
The table below gives the length $L$ (in cm) of a particular sunflower stem at various days $d$ after germination.

| $d$ | 10 | 15 | 20 | 25 | 30 | 35 | 40 |
|-----|----|----|----|----|----|----|----|
| $L(d)$ | 2.1 | 5.8 | 14.3 | 28.0 | 41.5 | 48.7 | 51.2 |

**(a)** Find the average growth rate (cm/day) over $[10, 40]$.

**(b)** Find the average growth rate over $[20, 30]$.

**(c)** During which 5-day interval is the average growth rate greatest?

**(d)** Biologists call the period of most rapid growth the "grand period of growth." Based on the data, when does it occur for this sunflower?

A population of yeast cells in a laboratory culture is measured every two hours:

| $t$ (hours) | 0 | 2 | 4 | 6 | 8 | 10 |
|-------------|---|---|---|---|---|----|
| $P(t)$ (cells) | 50 | 72 | 110 | 182 | 320 | 570 |

**(a)** Find the average growth rate from $t=0$ to $t=10$.

**(b)** Find the average growth rate over each 2-hour sub-interval.

**(c)** The growth appears to be accelerating. Is the average rate of change itself increasing? How can you tell?

**(d)** Estimate the instantaneous growth rate at $t = 4$ by averaging the rates over $[2,4]$ and $[4,6]$.

The distance (in km) of the planet Mars from the Sun at various points in its orbit can be approximated from Kepler's model. If the orbital position angle (in radians) is $\theta$ and the distance function is $r(\theta) = \dfrac{228}{1 + 0.0934\cos\theta}$ (in millions of km), find the average rate of change of distance with respect to angle from $\theta = 0$ to $\theta = \pi/2$, and from $\theta = \pi/2$ to $\theta = \pi$. Interpret your results physically.
